---
title: "Tutorial 2: ECCO Heat Transport, with a SWOT Coda"
subtitle: "Real NASA ocean data: meridional heat transport at 26.5N against RAPID, plus a SWOT SSH scene (~20 minutes)"
format: html
---

<!-- Surfaces verified: Claude Code 2026-07-05 (fresh data directory,
     timed: 15.0 minutes end to end incl. ~1.3 GB downloads). Cowork and Claude Science:
     pending; on surfaces without Earthdata credentials the workflow
     runs through planning and gate presentation, which is the designed
     behavior. -->

**Surfaces verified:** Claude Code (2026-07-05). Cowork and Claude
Science pending: without Earthdata credentials those surfaces exercise
planning, gates, and reporting; the downloads and computation are
Code-side.

## What you will do

Configure a project for ocean work, load real ECCO v4r4 data from
PO.DAAC through the volume gate, compute Atlantic meridional heat
transport at 26.5N with honest uncertainty framing, see the overturning
structure, get a report with provenance, and load a SWOT swath scene.
You need a (free) [Earthdata Login](https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov) in
`~/.netrc` and the ocean-science plugin:

```bash
claude plugin install ocean-science@open-science-pillars
```

## 1. Local config (2 minutes)

Copy the template into your project and fill the data paths:

> Set up an ocean-science local config here: ECCO data under
> ~/ECCO_V4r4, 2 GB download gate, North Atlantic working box.

The file it writes (`ocean-science.local.md`) is where the volume
gate's threshold and your data directories live; loaders read it.

## 2. Load ECCO through the gate (4 minutes)

> Load the ECCO 3D temperature fluxes and grid geometry for 2010.

What to watch: the request resolves to EXACT PO.DAAC ShortNames; the
granule count and volume appear BEFORE any download (about 770 MB for
this request, under your gate, so it proceeds with a statement); the
grid geometry is auto-merged; and the summary restates the knowledge
that applies (ECCO has no formal error fields; budgets need snapshots).
The fresh pull of the 2010 fluxes plus geometry (about 800 MB) took
under a minute on 2026-07-05; PO.DAAC's cloud egress is quick.

## 3. Heat transport at 26.5N (6 minutes)

> What is the Atlantic meridional heat transport at 26.5N in 2010, and
> how does it compare with expectations?

This is the tutorial's centerpiece, and it teaches the field's
sharpest scope trap:

- **Basin scope matters.** A latitude line without a basin mask is the
  FULL circle, all oceans. The plugin's recipe records both 2010
  anchors: Atlantic 0.666 PW, global circle 1.098 PW, and their sum
  identity with the Pacific and Indian pieces. The RAPID-comparable
  number is the Atlantic one. (This distinction was caught by the
  project's own verification loop; the recipe documents the whole
  story.)
- **Expectations come from a validated recipe, not from memory:** the
  multi-year Atlantic band (0.8-1.4 PW) and the RAPID comparison
  spread (about +/-0.4 PW) are quoted with citations, and 2010 sits
  low because it is the documented AMOC-minimum year, which the
  analysis says rather than hides.
- **Uncertainty framing:** ECCO ships no formal errors; the honest
  envelope is the recipe's comparison spread, and the response says so
  in as many words.

Then see the vertical structure:

> Show me the overturning streamfunction at 26.5N for 2010.

## 4. The report (3 minutes)

> Write this up as a report.

The confirmation gate shows you the filename and sections BEFORE
anything is written; the Results carry uncertainty statements on every
headline number; Provenance cites the knowledge concepts consulted by
bundle path. That report structure is enforced, not aspirational: the
project's eval suite tests it.

## 5. SWOT coda (4 minutes)

> Load a SWOT sea surface height scene for the Gulf Stream from March
> 2024 and summarize it.

Watch three things: the volume gate presents granule counts and sizes
first (a 3-day regional box is a handful of granules: ~9 MB each in
the Basic tier, ~33 MB in Expert); the
quality flags are decoded before any statistic (large masked
fractions over a swath are NORMAL and the summary says why); the swath
structure is preserved, nadir gap and all; and on current baselines the
crossover calibration (height_cor_xover) is applied to ssha before
statistics, per the dataset concept's known issue: uncorrected swath
statistics carry a spurious cross-track ramp of order meters.

**The orbit-phase note:** if your date range touches 2023, expect the
assistant to stop you: SWOT flew a 1-day cal/val orbit until July 2023
and the 21-day science orbit after, the two are not one record, and
the cal/val data lives only in the Version D collections. That
interruption is the knowledge layer working.

## 6. Where to go next

Ask your own question end to end ("How did the AMOC minimum of 2010
look in ECCO versus RAPID?") and watch the plan-load-analyze-report
pipeline run with the same guardrails. The knowledge bundle behind all
of it, with evidence links on every claim, is in the
[ocean-science repository](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/ocean-science).

**Timing note:** executed fresh (empty data directory, plugins
reinstalled) in 15.0 minutes on 2026-07-05 (measured), dominated by
downloads and the transport computation.
